evidence: Package layers are api → query/exploration/config → model/utils with no visible cycles; CollocateQueryHelper and BlackLabSnippetParser are correctly package-private to the query package.

SemanticFieldExplorer (exploration) hardcodes corpus-format-specific tagset patterns: private static final String NOUN_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]"; these are corpus-configuration decisions that cross the boundary between exploration logic and the corpus indexing format contract.
AdjectiveCollocateRanker also hardcodes private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]"; — same tagset coupling into the exploration subsystem.

Main.handleServerCommand explicitly handles a missing grammar config with a user-friendly message 'No grammar config found, using defaults' yet sets grammarConfig = null and immediately passes it to new WordSketchApiServer(executor, port, grammarConfig), whose constructor calls Objects.requireNonNull — the module boundary contract is violated, producing a silent runtime crash rather than the advertised graceful fallback.

WordSketchApiServer private methods registerGetHandler and registerPostHandler accept com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer as a parameter, binding the internal JDK HTTP server type into the class's own structural seams even though no external caller sees this directly.

Package-to-domain mapping is generally clear: api (HTTP), config (grammar), exploration (semantic analysis), indexer (corpus ingestion), model (DTOs), query (corpus search), utils (shared utilities), viz (SVG rendering).

AdjectiveCollocateRanker Javadoc: 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold.' — decomposition driven by mechanical line count, not cohesion; the ranker is always used through a one-line delegation in SemanticFieldExplorer.

model package mixes two conventions without consistency: AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, ExploreOptions, Edge are records (component-style accessors); ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult are classes with getX() JavaBeans accessors. Each has a Javadoc comment explaining the rationale, but this requires a developer to read two different access patterns in the same package.

ExplorationHandlers contains 7+ private static formatXxx methods (formatSeedCollocate, formatDiscoveredNoun, formatCoreCollocate, formatEdge, formatAdjectiveProfile) that serialize domain objects into Map<String,Object>. This serialization concern co-habitates with HTTP routing in the handler.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType has an isDep boolean that branches throughout the method to produce two structurally similar but differently shaped responses — the method owns two different logical paths.

WordSketchApiServer constructor has a TODO comment: 'Move HttpServer creation and route registration here so start() only calls server.start()... defer to v2.' The current split between constructor and start() is a known design shortcut left in production code.
RelationUtils is a small package-private class mapping alias strings to canonical IDs — a clean, well-scoped helper.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles delegates entirely to AdjectiveCollocateRanker.compareCollocateProfiles in a single line: return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seeds, minLogDice, topCollocates); — pure pass-through with no added policy.
QueryExecutor is an interface with 6 methods, one implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) in production. Testability justifies the interface but it is a single-implementation contract throughout.

ExplorationHandlers contains 7+ private static formatXxx(DomainObject) → Map<String,Object> methods. These are unnamed serializers embedded inside a handler class rather than defined on the domain types or a dedicated serializer.

SemanticFieldExplorer hardcodes NOUN_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]"; and AdjectiveCollocateRanker hardcodes ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]"; — config-layer decisions embedded as implementation constants.

CollocateQueryHelper is a well-fitted package-private abstraction: it extracts cohesive collocate-search-and-rank logic shared across three methods of BlackLabQueryExecutor, adding real value by consolidating frequency lookup and logDice scoring.
BlackLabSnippetParser is a cohesive static utility class extracting all XML/text parsing from BlackLabQueryExecutor and CollocateQueryHelper; it pays for itself across multiple calling sites.
LongIntHashMap provides a boxing-free long→int map for performance-critical collocate counting; the custom primitive implementation is justified by the specific performance need in corpus processing.
ExploreOptions record bundles 5 parameters that would otherwise appear on every exploration method signature — appropriate parameter-object pattern.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens and PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate together form a small, coherent CQL-manipulation layer serving both config loading and concordance example building.

pom.xml declares 5 production dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser, fastjson2, slf4j-api, logback-classic. Test dependencies are JUnit 5 (api, engine, platform-launcher). No duplication or alternative libraries for the same purpose.

fastjson2 is used consistently for both JSON serialization (JSON.toJSONString in HttpApiUtils) and deserialization (JSON.parseObject in SketchHandlers, GrammarConfigLoader). Single library, no mixing with Gson/Jackson/etc.

blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser are both required: the engine handles index open/search operations, the parser handles CQL and BCQL query parsing. These are distinct modules of the BlackLab framework, not alternatives.

BlackLab 4.0.0 is resolved from a non-standard repository: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/ (GitHub raw URL). This is a raw file server, not a proper Maven repository, creating a build reproducibility risk if the GitHub repository moves, is renamed, or the raw URL structure changes.

com.sun.net.httpserver is used as the HTTP server throughout the api package. This is a JDK-internal package available in JDK 6+ but not on the official javax.* or jakarta.* module paths; it is not a Maven dependency but represents an implicit platform-tier dependency on an internal JDK API.
SLF4J 2.0.7 paired with logback-classic 1.5.18: SLF4J 2.x requires logback 1.4+, so this pairing is correct and compatible.
JUnit Platform 1.9.0 paired with JUnit Jupiter 5.9.0: these correspond to the same BOM version, no misalignment.
No version ranges or SNAPSHOT dependencies that could cause non-reproducible builds (all versions are pinned constants).

Main.handleServerCommand: the catch branch for a missing grammar config prints 'No grammar config found at ..., using defaults.' but leaves grammarConfig = null, which is then passed to new WordSketchApiServer(executor, port, grammarConfig) where Objects.requireNonNull throws NPE. The control flow communicates graceful fallback but executes a crash.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore unpacks ExploreParams into four local variables (topCollocates, minShared, minLogDice, nounsPerSeed) immediately after calling resolveExploreParams, then re-packages them into ExploreOptions in the next line. The intermediate variables are noise — ExploreOptions could be built directly from the record.

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits: int limit = Math.min(records.size(), maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR); — OVER_FETCH_FACTOR is 3, so if maxResults is near Integer.MAX_VALUE / 3, the multiplication overflows silently. The cast to long is missing.

LongIntHashMap: EMPTY_KEY = Long.MIN_VALUE is the sentinel for an unused slot, but the public put(long, int) and get(long) methods do not document that Long.MIN_VALUE cannot be stored. A caller who stores Long.MIN_VALUE will corrupt the map silently.

GrammarConfigLoader.throwForMissingConfig is a void private method whose only behavior is throwing an exception; its name does not signal this (compare: throwIfMissing, ensureExists). The method is called unconditionally after a conditional check, so it always throws when called — the name should reflect that.

BlackLabSnippetParser.findLabelPosition uses a manual character scan with nested depth tracking; the method mixes two concerns: finding '[' character count (for position) and finding the labeled token offset. The increment logic has a subtle issue: when ch == '[', depth is incremented and then i is incremented again at the bottom of the while loop, meaning a '[' that opens a block is counted and then the inner loop also advances i, which can cause correct behavior in simple cases but is fragile for patterns with nested brackets.

CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits constructs records.add(new HitRecord(...)) with empty strings in two null-guard paths before the main extraction; the null-guard paths are correct but the HitRecord constructor call is repeated identically twice — extract a helper or use a single guarded-return flow.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern has a well-structured four-phase decomposition (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounCollocateMap, scoreAndFilterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) with clearly named helper methods for each phase.
ExploreOptions record cleanly bundles 4 numeric tuning parameters, preventing parameter-list explosion at the handler→explorer seam
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias is the single translation point for alias → grammar-config-ID mapping; both ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers use it without reimplementing
buildExploreResponseBody() is shared between handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, so the JSON serialization seam is defined once

ExplorationResult used as the output contract for both exploreByPattern and exploreMultiSeed, but with incompatible semantics: in single-seed mode discoveredNouns contains nouns discovered in the field; in multi-seed mode discoveredNouns contains the seeds themselves
ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed is unused in the multi-seed call path (confirmed by in-code comment 'nouns_per intentionally not supported in multi-seed mode')

handleFullSketchForType uses a boolean isDep flag to fork both query dispatch and response structure — the DEP path adds a 'type':'dependency' key and uses buildRelationResponse(), the SURFACE path builds an anonymous map inline

CORS origin 'http://localhost:3000' is hardcoded in 4 separate HttpApiUtils methods (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse) — the origin value is a cross-cutting boundary policy repeated rather than centralized

GrammarConfigLoader has Javadoc on 8+ private/trivial methods that restate the method name: readConfigFile ('Reads config file content, throwing IOException if the file does not exist'), throwForMissingConfig ('Always throws IllegalStateException for a missing config file'), parseAndValidateVersion ('Extracts and validates the version field from the root JSON object'), getRelations ('Get all configured relations'), getRelation ('Get a relation by ID'), getVersion ('Get the configuration version'), getConfigPath ('Get the config file path')
RelationConfig is a record with 'private static final Logger logger = LoggerFactory.getLogger(RelationConfig.class)' — this logger is declared but never called anywhere in the file
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateLemma Javadoc contains '{@link #extractLemmaAt(String, int)}' referencing a method that does not exist in the class
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractPosFromMatch has inline comments '// Try xpos first' and '// Fallback to upos' that restate exactly what the code below them does with no additional context

BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations: 'if (identity == null || identity.isEmpty())' null-guards group.identity().toString() — toString() on a non-null HitGroup identity cannot return null under BlackLab's API contract

CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, QueryExecutor, PatternSubstitution all have appropriately substantive comments explaining non-obvious constraints, algorithm choices, and format conventions — these are genuine why-comments

RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint contains dual support: a primary xpos= block and a secondary tag= block explicitly labeled '// legacy: tag= attribute support — use xpos= in new grammars'. The two code paths coexist with no removal plan or deprecation timeline

GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys actively rejects the 'copulas' key with a clear migration error message: 'Grammar config must NOT contain copulas key... Copulas must be embedded in CQL patterns' — this is a completed migration with hard enforcement
GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelation comment: '// Canonical field name is "pattern"; cql_pattern is no longer supported' — the old field name was removed cleanly with no compatibility shim

BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution handles two CQL template formats: %s-style substitution AND [constraint]-prefix-appended format. Both branches are active; no comment indicates which is the new or old form

WordSketchApiServer constructor has TODO(595bb84c): 'Move HttpServer creation and route registration here so start() only calls server.start(). Requires declaring IOException on this constructor, which is a breaking-API change — defer to v2.'

Main.java uses command names 'blacklab-index', 'blacklab-query' — the 'blacklab-' prefix is retained even though the indexer is now the only implementation, suggesting the names are a migration vestige from when a non-BlackLab path may have existed

Directory structure: api/ (5 files), config/ (4 files), exploration/ (2 files), indexer/blacklab/ (2 files), model/ (8 files), query/ (4 files), tools/ (empty), utils/ (3 files), viz/ (1 file), root Main.java

PatternSubstitution.java in utils/ implements substituteCollocate(): inserts a lemma constraint at a given token position. RelationConfig.java in config/ implements mergeLemmaConstraint() and substituteHeadword() — structurally identical operations (bracket-walk, extract existing xpos constraint, build new [lemma=x & xpos=y] token). Same algorithm, different packages
viz/ package contains exactly one file: RadialPlot.java. All callers (SketchHandlers) are in api/. A single-class package with no sibling classes is a package that exists only to hold one file
tools/ directory exists in the source tree but contains no .java files

model/ package contains QueryResults.java which houses 4 inner types (WordSketchResult, ConcordanceResult, SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult). The ConcordanceResult sealed interface and its subtypes are tightly used by the query layer but are placed in model/

exploration/ package contains SemanticFieldExplorer and AdjectiveCollocateRanker — both correctly placed as domain orchestration above the query layer. AdjectiveCollocateRanker was extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer specifically to manage class size, which is explicitly noted in its Javadoc
config/ contains GrammarConfigLoader, RelationConfig, RelationType, PosGroup — all grammar-configuration types. Clean cohesion.
api/ contains WordSketchApiServer, SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, HttpApiUtils, RelationUtils — all HTTP boundary concerns. Clean cohesion.

Main.java: 'catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("No grammar config found at '" + grammarConfigPath + "', using defaults."); }' — then immediately calls new WordSketchApiServer(executor, port, grammarConfig) where grammarConfig is still null. WordSketchApiServer constructor calls Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig) which throws NullPointerException. The 'using defaults' message actively misrepresents the outcome.

ExplorationResult.getSeed() returns String.join(',', seeds) in exploreMultiSeed: 'return new ExplorationResult(String.join(",", seeds), ...)'. getSeed() is named as a single-lemma accessor but returns 'theory,model,hypothesis' in multi-seed mode.

ComparisonResult.getEdges() iterates over all adjectives and builds a new ArrayList on every invocation — computation hidden as a get* accessor. ExplorationResult.getEdges() similarly builds new lists from seedCollocates and discoveredNouns on each call.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(String, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) declares 'throws IOException' but delegates to collocateReversePattern() which throws IllegalStateException — an unchecked exception that propagates invisibly to callers expecting only IOException failures.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern: contract says 'collocate position is inferred from the 2: label in bcqlPattern' but when findLabelPosition returns -1 (label absent), both extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition and extractPlainTextTokenAt return null for position -1, the freqMap stays empty, and an empty list is returned with no error signal.

QueryExecutor interface has carefully differentiated Javadoc for findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern — these are genuine contract-level distinctions that callers rely on.
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() throws IllegalStateException with a detailed message when relationType is absent — the precondition failure is explicit and informative.

SketchHandlers constructor accepts grammarConfig without a null check, but WordSketchApiServer wraps it with requireNonNull before constructing SketchHandlers, so the effective null gate is at the right level.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType: response.put("relations", byRelation) appears in both the isDep=true and isDep=false branches; the else branch is identical to the then-branch minus the extra 'type' key, so the common put could be lifted above the conditional.

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits: the guard `collocateLemma != null && !collocateLemma.isEmpty()` is evaluated twice in immediate succession within the same loop iteration — first for the jointFreq lookup block and again for the collocateFreq ternary.

SemanticFieldExplorer: an orphaned Javadoc block (describing a 5-parameter signature: seed, relationName, bcqlPattern, simplePattern, opts) immediately precedes a second Javadoc block that attaches to the 3-parameter overload (seed, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions). Java associates only the immediately preceding Javadoc with a declaration, so the first block documents nothing and misleadingly describes parameters that do not match the method that follows.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti caps minShared to seeds.size() before calling exploreMultiSeed; exploreMultiSeed then re-caps the same value again with `int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seeds.size())`. Both clamps produce the same result, obscuring which site owns the constraint.

RadialPlot.toSVG() recomputes centerX, centerY, and scale from scratch using the same formulas that the constructor already evaluated; since these values are not stored as fields there is no single source of truth for these layout constants.

Edge.type is declared as String; values 'seed_adj', 'discovered_adj', 'modifier' are hardcoded at every call site with no compile-time validation. An EdgeType enum exists nowhere in the codebase but the values are functionally an enumeration.
All API response payloads are constructed as Map<String, Object>. Key typos and wrong value types (e.g., putting a List where a String is expected) are not caught until runtime serialisation.

HitRecord (package-private record in CollocateQueryHelper) declares `String collocateLemma` which is explicitly set to null at multiple construction sites, yet the record component type does not communicate nullability. Callers must null-check but the type provides no contract.

RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES keys are raw String literals ('adj_predicate', 'adj_modifier', etc.) with no tie to any enum constant. A misspelled alias returns the input unchanged (pass-through), silently failing at the downstream config lookup rather than at the alias definition site.

ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig explicitly validates that relationType() is non-empty before returning the config, but both callers then immediately call resolvedConfig.relationType().orElseThrow() — trusting validation they already performed via a redundant Optional unwrap that adds no safety.

SketchHandlers.handleVisualRadial calls obj.getIntValue("width") twice: once to test == 0 and once to read the value. A legitimate width of 0 (edge case, but valid) is misclassified as 'not provided' and silently replaced with 840.

SemanticFieldExplorer declares `private final AdjectiveCollocateRanker comparator` — the field name 'comparator' suggests a java.util.Comparator-like role, but the object is a domain-specific collocate ranker that profiles and ranks adjective distributions across nouns. The name/type mismatch is confusing when reading SemanticFieldExplorer code.

BlackLabQueryExecutor declares `private final CollocateQueryHelper helper` — 'helper' communicates no behaviour or responsibility. The type name CollocateQueryHelper already encodes the domain; the field should carry enough of that meaning to be readable without inspecting the type declaration.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison uses `String nounsParam = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(exchange, params, "seeds")` — the variable is named 'nounsParam' but the HTTP parameter key is 'seeds'. The divergence means anyone searching for 'seeds' in the handler code will not find the local variable by name.

QueryExecutor interface mixes `findCollocations` (find-prefix, returns collocates ranked by logDice) with `executeSurfacePattern` (execute-prefix, also returns collocates ranked by logDice). Both methods serve the same caller purpose — collocate extraction — but their names imply different abstraction levels, making it non-obvious which to use for a new caller.

SemanticFieldExplorer.buildNounCollocateMap performs a reverse lookup: for each collocate, it queries what nouns co-occur with it. The name suggests building a noun→collocate map (forward direction) but the actual algorithm is: for each collocate, find its nouns (reverse direction). The naming hides the key algorithmic insight.

Main.handleServerCommand catches IOException from GrammarConfigLoader construction and prints 'No grammar config found...using defaults.' It then passes null to WordSketchApiServer whose constructor immediately calls Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig) — the IOException is swallowed and replaced with an NPE at the next line, and the user sees a misleading 'using defaults' message before a crash.

GrammarConfigLoader.readConfigFile declares throws IOException but throws IllegalStateException (unchecked) for the missing-file case via throwForMissingConfig. The checked/unchecked split is inconsistent and surprises callers who catch IOException expecting to handle the missing-file path.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType catches `Exception` broadly for each relation iteration and logs a warning without surfacing the partial-failure in the API response. The caller receives a 200 response with silently missing relations and no indication that an error occurred.

BlackLabConllUIndexer.errorOccurred returns true (continue indexing) after logging the error. The final document/token counts will be silently incorrect with no exception propagated to the caller and no error count exposed.

handleVisualRadial and handleBcqlQueryPost each have their own catch(Exception) blocks, and both are additionally wrapped by wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer which also catches IOException and Exception. The outer wrapper can only trigger for exceptions that escape the inner handlers, but the layering is asymmetric: handleVisualRadial logs and sends 500 in the inner catch, so the outer wrapper never fires — yet its presence creates a misleading impression of dual-layer protection.

HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams catches Exception broadly and logs a warning, silently dropping the malformed parameter. The caller receives an empty params map entry for that key without any indication that parsing failed, which can cause confusing 'missing required parameter' errors downstream.

RadialPlot declares three static final fields computed at class-load time: `private static final DecimalFormatSymbols dfs = DecimalFormatSymbols.getInstance(Locale.US); private static final DecimalFormat df = new DecimalFormat("0.00", dfs); private static final DecimalFormat dfInt = new DecimalFormat("0", dfs);` — DecimalFormat is explicitly NOT thread-safe per its Javadoc. The HTTP server may dispatch concurrent requests; concurrent calls to renderFromItems() → toSVG() → fmt() → df.format() will race on shared mutable formatter state.

WordSketchApiServer has a two-phase initialization: the constructor creates SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, and SemanticFieldExplorer but does NOT create the HttpServer. start() creates the HttpServer and registers routes. The field `private com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer server` is null from construction until start() is called. This implicit ordering is not enforced by the type system.

Main.handleServerCommand constructs a BlackLabQueryExecutor before constructing WordSketchApiServer, then adds a shutdown hook that calls executor.close(). If WordSketchApiServer construction throws (e.g., the grammarConfig NPE), the executor is leaked because the shutdown hook is not registered until after both constructions succeed — but the hook references a variable that may be partially initialized.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads `System.getProperty("grammar.config", "grammars/relations.json")` at call time. This is correct; however, the method is a static factory whose underlying path resolution depends on the JVM working directory at the time it is first called. Tests that change the working directory after class load would observe different behaviour from production, creating a fragile environment dependency.
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is a static final Map computed at class load. There is no external dependency, no I/O, and no mutable state — this is a safe static initializer.
Java naming conventions (camelCase methods, PascalCase classes, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE constants) are applied uniformly across all packages.
Package structure is consistent: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz — each with a single concern level.

Handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, RelationUtils) are package-private; public surface classes (WordSketchApiServer, HttpApiUtils, GrammarConfigLoader) are public — consistent with standard Java encapsulation.

model package mixes records (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, Edge, ExploreOptions) with a traditional JavaBeans class (ExplorationResult). The divergence is explicitly documented in ExplorationResult's Javadoc ('This is a traditional class (not a record)... because it is built incrementally') — intentional and documented.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType builds different-shaped relData maps for SURFACE vs DEP inside the same loop: SURFACE uses keys {name, cql, collocate_pos_group, collocations} while DEP (via buildRelationResponse) uses {id, name, description, deprel, total_matches, collocations}. The key for the CQL pattern string itself differs: 'cql' in SURFACE, absent in DEP (description is present instead). This is a style island within the handler response-building pattern.

SemanticFieldExplorer.java lines 100-107 contain an orphaned Javadoc fragment ('Convenience overload...') immediately followed by a second, properly-attached Javadoc block on the actual method (lines 108-119). The stale fragment is misleading.
All static utility classes (HttpApiUtils, CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, PatternSubstitution) consistently use private constructors to prevent instantiation.

20 test files exist covering: utilities (LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, PatternSubstitution, CqlUtils), config (GrammarConfigLoader, RelationsValidation), API handlers (HandlersTest, HttpApiUtilsTest, RelationUtilsTest), query components (BlackLabSnippetParserTest, CollocateQueryHelperTest, SemanticFieldExplorerTest, BCQLParserTest), and integration paths (BlackLabQueryExecutorTest, DependencySketchTest, HybridAlgorithmTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest).
SemanticFieldExplorerTest uses a StubExecutor implementing QueryExecutor — tests are decoupled from the real index for the exploration logic path.
HandlersTest uses a MockExchange and emptyExecutor() stub, testing handler validation logic without real HTTP or index dependencies.

Main.java CLI argument parsing (handleBlackLabIndexCommand, handleBlackLabQueryCommand, handleServerCommand) has no corresponding tests. Argument errors (e.g. missing --input) are handled with System.err.println and early returns — this critical entry-point code path is entirely untested.
ConlluConverter (CoNLL-U → WPL chunk conversion) has no test file. This is the corpus pre-processing step; bugs here corrupt the entire index silently.
RadialPlot (SVG generation, handleVisualRadial handler) has no test. The handleVisualRadial handler is also not covered in HandlersTest.
BlackLabDummyTest filename suggests it may be a minimal placeholder rather than a meaningful test.
CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits performs per-collocate frequency lookups in a loop (getTotalFrequency called per entry in freqMap): this hot-path logic is not directly unit-tested with mock data.

The 5-arg exploreByPattern overload (raw pattern strings) is directly called in SemanticFieldExplorerTest, which creates test-production coupling: if the public signature changes, tests break for an implementation-detail overload.
AdjectiveCollocateRanker has its own test file (AdjectiveCollocateRankerTest) — comparison logic has dedicated coverage.

POST /api/bcql response does not include a 'status' field. Every other endpoint (sketch, relations, semantic-field/explore, semantic-field, semantic-field/examples, concordance/examples, visual/radial error path) returns a top-level 'status: "ok"'. The BCQL response omits it, breaking the caller's ability to use a uniform success check.

QueryExecutor interface exposes two methods with overlapping semantics: findCollocations(lemma, cqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) and executeSurfacePattern(lemma, bcqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) both return List<WordSketchResult> for the same lemma+pattern combination. The distinction (CQL vs BCQL, labeled positions vs unlabeled) is documented in Javadoc but is not reflected in the method names or parameter types. Callers must read the interface contract to avoid mixing them.

handleSemanticFieldComparison (ExplorationHandlers, lines 144-199) inlines its own numeric parameter parsing: 'minLogDice = Double.parseDouble(...)' and 'topCollocates = Integer.parseInt(...)'. Its sibling handlers handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti delegate to resolveExploreParams(). The default values also diverge: 'top' defaults to '50' in comparison vs '15' in resolveExploreParams.

The semantic-field comparison endpoint (/api/semantic-field) response omits 'relation_type' and 'parameters' keys that both explore endpoints include. All three are sibling semantic-field endpoints but their response envelopes are shaped differently.

executeBcqlQuery and executeQuery on QueryExecutor both accept a bcql/cql pattern and a limit, but differ in return type (CollocateResult vs ConcordanceResult) with no type-level signal to the caller. The names 'executeQuery' vs 'executeBcqlQuery' do not clearly indicate that the parsers (ContextualQueryLanguageParser vs CorpusQueryLanguageParser) are different.

handleConcordanceExamples (SketchHandlers) silently falls back to a proximity pattern when the named relation is not found, returning 'fallback: true' in the response. No other endpoint has a fallback mode. Callers cannot distinguish a correct relation-matched result from an approximate proximity result without inspecting the 'fallback' field.

WordSketchApiServer.registerGetHandler and registerPostHandler are private helpers that mirror each other exactly except for the method string. This structural duplication could be unified into a single registerHandler(path, allowedMethod, handler) method.

The server binds exclusively to the loopback address: 'new InetSocketAddress(InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress(), port)' (WordSketchApiServer.java line 94). This is the primary network-level access control and is consistently applied at startup.

No authentication or authorisation framework exists. All endpoints are equally open to any process on the local machine. This is consistent (no endpoint has auth while a sibling does not) but means any local process can issue arbitrary queries.

CORS is hardcoded to 'http://localhost:3000' across sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, and sendBinaryResponse in HttpApiUtils — four separate call-sites all carry the same string literal rather than a named constant. The comment 'Dev-only: restrict to specific origins in production' appears four times with no enforcement mechanism.

POST /api/bcql accepts an arbitrary BCQL query string from the request body and passes it directly to executor.executeBcqlQuery() without any sanitisation, pattern restriction, or query complexity limit. All structured endpoints (sketch, explore, etc.) validate the relation ID against grammarConfig before constructing a query; the BCQL endpoint bypasses this.

Lemma values from URL path segments and query parameters are passed through CqlUtils.escapeForRegex() before being embedded in CQL patterns (RelationConfig.getFullPattern → mergeLemmaConstraint). This escaping is applied consistently for structured endpoints.

Relation IDs from query parameters are validated via grammarConfig.getRelation(relationId).isEmpty() before use — unknown relations return 400. This input trust boundary is consistently applied across all structured endpoints.
Numeric parameters (top, min_logdice, limit, max) are individually parsed with try/catch and return 400 on NumberFormatException — consistent across all handlers.
No rate limiting, request size cap, or timeout is applied to any endpoint. A caller can issue unlimited requests or a very complex BCQL pattern without constraint.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType (lines 114-162): a boolean flag 'isDep' is passed into the method and threads through the entire body. Inside the per-relation loop, the response map is built with completely different keys for SURFACE vs DEP. The method handles two distinct responsibilities (surface sketch formatting, DEP sketch formatting) in a single body, controlled by a boolean rather than dispatched polymorphically.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison (lines 144-199) does not reuse the resolveExploreParams helper that its sibling handlers use, and instead inlines the same parsing logic with different defaults. This creates a silent divergence in default values ('top' is 50 here vs 15 in resolveExploreParams).

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern has two overloads: a 3-arg (seed, relationConfig, opts) convenience method and a 5-arg (seed, relationName, bcqlPattern, simplePattern, opts) implementation method. The 5-arg overload is public despite being an implementation detail — it exposes raw pattern strings that are already encapsulated in RelationConfig. The 3-arg overload is the correct public API.

CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates (lines 124-151) calls getTotalFrequency(collocateLemma) for each entry in freqMap inside a loop, making O(N) index lookups per scoring call. This is a design-level concern: the collocate frequencies could be batch-fetched or pre-computed at the search stage rather than per-entry in the ranking stage.

SemanticFieldExplorer.buildNounCollocateMap (lines 221-237) calls executor.findCollocations once per entry in seedCollocScores — an O(N) loop of index queries. N can be up to topPredicates (default 15). This is an unavoidable architectural cost of the two-phase exploration algorithm, but the method makes no attempt to short-circuit on empty results or parallelize, which would be a straightforward improvement.

AdjectiveCollocateRanker.compareCollocateProfiles and SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed share the same two-phase pattern: (1) iterate seeds, fetch collocates, accumulate a collocateSharedCount map; (2) threshold and collect common collocates. The structural pattern is duplicated without a shared abstraction.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType (line 156-162): the DEP path sets response.put("type", "dependency") while the SURFACE path sets nothing for type — an implicit 'surface' type. If both paths go through the same formatter, the type should be explicit in both.

GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition (lines 260-302): 40-line character-scanning loop that is a second complete implementation of the bracket-walking logic already in CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens. The method itself notes this in a Javadoc comment ('Extends the bracket-walking in CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens') but the logic is not actually shared — it is a parallel copy with an added label-prefix detection feature.

strengths: Package-to-layer mapping is coherent: api, query, exploration, config, model, utils, viz are distinct with no observed import cycles.
CollocateQueryHelper and BlackLabSnippetParser are package-private, correctly hiding BlackLab internals from the API and exploration layers.
The sealed QueryResults hierarchy (SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult) provides an honest, bounded type boundary between the query layer and its consumers.
LongIntHashMap is self-contained in utils with no upward dependencies; RadialPlot is similarly isolated with no domain coupling.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at subsystem boundaries rather than scattered: two exploration-layer classes directly encode corpus-format assumptions that belong in config, and a cross-boundary null contract between Main and WordSketchApiServer creates a crash that contradicts user-visible messaging.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase sits above 60 (significant drag) because the overall layer structure is sound and internal encapsulation is consistently applied. It falls short of 80 (solid but uneven) due to the hardcoded tagset patterns that cross the config/exploration boundary — a developer adding a new corpus format would need to edit exploration internals — and the null-grammarConfig contract violation, which is a real trap at the Main-to-API module seam.

strengths: Package boundaries map to domain capabilities without historical accidents: api, config, exploration, model, query, viz are all independently navigable.
The model package clearly owns all domain result types; callers in api and exploration import from model without circular ownership.
RelationUtils is a minimal, well-scoped alias resolver that keeps relation-name logic out of the handler bodies.
QueryExecutor's Javadoc precisely distinguishes findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, and executeSurfacePattern, making the public surface understandable.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues stem from incomplete separation of serialization from routing in the API layer and a size-driven decomposition of the exploration subsystem that obscures semantic ownership.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase scores above 60 because the domain-to-package mapping is coherent and model ownership is predictable. It falls short of 80 because AdjectiveCollocateRanker is a mechanical size-relief extraction rather than a genuinely independent concept, the model package requires developers to switch between two accessor conventions, and ExplorationHandlers conflates HTTP response serialization with handler routing, blurring what a 'handler' owns.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper extracts real shared logic — frequency lookup, grouped search, and logDice ranking — from three callers in BlackLabQueryExecutor, paying for the abstraction with genuine deduplication.
LongIntHashMap is a purposeful custom data structure for boxing-free collocate frequency accumulation; the trade-off is explicitly stated.
ExploreOptions bundles exploration parameters into a named type, eliminating a 5-argument method signature.
BlackLabSnippetParser consolidates all XML/text extraction into one place used by both CollocateQueryHelper and BlackLabQueryExecutor.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The main abstraction fitness problem is pass-through delegation: SemanticFieldExplorer wraps AdjectiveCollocateRanker without adding policy, and response formatting logic is scattered as anonymous static helpers inside handler classes rather than living on domain types.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase scores above 60 because core infrastructure abstractions (CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, LongIntHashMap, ExploreOptions) are well-fitted. It falls short of 70 due to the one-line delegation passthrough in SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles, the hardcoded tagset patterns that should be config-sourced, and the informal serialization helpers embedded in handler classes that prevent the domain model from being self-describing.

strengths: Dependency set is minimal: 5 production libraries, all actively used throughout the codebase.
Single JSON library (fastjson2) used consistently for both serialization and deserialization with no alternatives.
All versions are pinned constants — no ranges or SNAPSHOTs that could cause non-reproducible builds.
SLF4J and logback versions are compatible (SLF4J 2.x + logback 1.4+).
dependency_health.issue_character: Issues are limited to a single external availability risk (non-standard BlackLab repository) and an implicit reliance on a JDK-internal HTTP server API, neither of which affect day-to-day build behavior but both create future brittleness.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set scores well above 60 because it is lean, consistent, and correctly versioned with no duplicate libraries. It falls short of 80 because BlackLab 4.0.0 is sourced from a raw GitHub URL rather than Maven Central or a proper artifact repository, and the implicit reliance on com.sun.net.httpserver ties the runtime to an internal JDK API that could be restricted in future Java versions.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer's four-phase exploration pipeline (fetch collocates → build noun map → score candidates → identify core) is clearly decomposed with one private method per phase, each with a focused responsibility.
BlackLabSnippetParser collects all regex Pattern constants as pre-compiled static finals at the top of the class, avoiding repeated compilation in hot paths.

LogDiceCalculator uses a named UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel (Double.NaN) with documentation directing callers to use Double.isNaN(), correctly distinguishing the uncomputable case from a legitimate zero score.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are a mix of one correctness-affecting control-flow problem (null grammarConfig crash) and several precision/clarity issues: redundant variable unpacking, an integer overflow potential in an over-fetch multiplier, and an undocumented sentinel collision in LongIntHashMap.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits near 60 (significant drag). The exploration pipeline and snippet-parsing code are well-structured with clear extraction. However, the grammarConfig null control-flow crash in Main is a direct, observable bug that contradicts explicit user-facing messaging; the LongIntHashMap sentinel collision is an undocumented correctness trap; and the integer overflow in the over-fetch limit calculation is a silent correctness risk. These are substantive, not stylistic, issues.

strengths: ExploreOptions record as a parameter-bundle eliminates a 5-arg method signature at the handler→explorer boundary
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias provides a single, explicit translation seam between HTTP alias strings and grammar-config IDs
buildExploreResponseBody() and buildBaseExploreResponse() factored as shared formatters, preventing response-shape duplication across single/multi-seed handlers
HttpApiUtils provides a minimal, consistent boundary layer: parseQueryParams, requireParam, sendJsonResponse cover the shared path without overfitting to any single handler
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The dominant pattern is semantic overloading: ExplorationResult is reused for two structurally-different modes (single-seed discovery vs. multi-seed comparison), silently changing what discoveredNouns means and making the shared buildExploreResponseBody seam work by coincidence rather than contract.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The handler→service→query layering is largely clean and the seam utilities are well-extracted. However, ExplorationResult's dual-mode overload is a genuine seam design flaw — callers cannot tell from the type what the discoveredNouns represent, and ExploreOptions carries a dead field in one mode. The isDep flag splitting response structure inside one method adds a second seam impurity. These are concentrated but impactful enough to place the codebase below a 70 on this dimension.

strengths: Domain-specific terminology used throughout: lemma, collocate, logDice, deprel, xpos — no generic handleData or processItem names
QueryExecutor interface comments precisely distinguish overlapping methods (findCollocations vs executeBcqlQuery vs executeSurfacePattern), adding genuine contractual value
LogDiceCalculator documents the formula, range, and sentinel value with mathematical precision — not restating code
CollocateQueryHelper phase-labeling comments (Phase 1/2/3) add algorithmic structure that the code alone does not make explicit
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The AI debt is concentrated in documentation mass: trivial private methods in GrammarConfigLoader are over-documented with restating Javadoc, and a hallucinated @link reference in BlackLabSnippetParser indicates a generation artifact that was never verified against the actual API.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase avoids the worst AI debt patterns (entry/exit logging, pass-through wrappers, generic names). However, GrammarConfigLoader systematically over-documents its internal implementation details with restating Javadocs, and the unused Logger field in a record plus the hallucinated method reference are clear AI-generation artifacts that were never caught in review. This places the codebase in the 45-60 range — worse than a carefully hand-written codebase but without the most egregious patterns.

strengths: cql_pattern field rename to pattern was completed cleanly with no backward-compat code — the old name is simply rejected
copulas key migration enforced at load time with a clear, actionable error message guiding the user to the new pattern
No old-and-new API coexistence at the query execution or exploration layers — there is exactly one executor implementation and one exploration path
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The migration issues are narrow and deliberate: the legacy tag= attribute fallback in posGroupFromConstraint is the only live dead-end, while other apparent migration artifacts (validateNoLegacyKeys, the cql_pattern comment) are clean migration guards rather than incomplete transitions.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase shows signs of careful cleanup — copulas removal, cql_pattern removal — but the tag= legacy path remains open indefinitely and the dual CQL template format in buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution has no clear target state. These are small, contained issues. Compared to codebases with pervasive old+new coexistence, this scores in the upper-middle range.

strengths: api/, config/, exploration/, query/ are all semantically coherent — each package's name accurately reflects its contents
exploration/ correctly isolates domain orchestration (SemanticFieldExplorer, AdjectiveCollocateRanker) from HTTP handling and query execution
indexer/blacklab/ nesting isolates the BlackLab-specific indexing concern with clear ownership
model/ as a pure-DTO package (all records and simple classes, no business logic) follows the standard layered architecture convention
package_organization.issue_character: The main organizational deficiency is fragmented ownership of CQL substitution logic: the same structural operation (lemma injection into a CQL token) is independently implemented in both utils/PatternSubstitution and config/RelationConfig, and the single-file viz/ package and empty tools/ package add navigational noise without corresponding cohesion.
package_organization.score_rationale: For a medium-sized Java project (~30 classes), the package decomposition is clear and mostly purposeful. The primary issue — split CQL substitution logic across utils/ and config/ — is a real cohesion problem that makes it non-obvious where to find or modify this concern. The thin viz/ package and orphaned tools/ directory add minor navigational friction. This places the codebase in the 65-75 range: organized enough for easy navigation on the main paths, but with identified fragmentation.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface clearly documents the behavioral distinctions between its four similar methods, making the contract explicit for implementors and callers
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() uses fail-fast IllegalStateException with a descriptive message rather than returning null or a sentinel
GrammarConfigLoader uses IOException with actionable messages for all config validation failures, maintaining a consistent error contract across the parsing pipeline
contract_coherence.issue_character: The most severe contract violation is in Main.java where the error message promises graceful fallback ('using defaults') but the actual code path throws; the remaining issues are subtler — getter methods concealing computation, a multi-mode method returning structurally different values from a single-valued accessor, and undocumented unchecked exceptions on a checked-exception method.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The contract violations are not pervasive but they are consequential: the 'using defaults' lie in Main.java will mislead any operator trying to run without a grammar config, and the ExplorationResult.getSeed() multi-mode anomaly creates a silent mismatch in any code path that calls getSeed() on a multi-seed result. The getEdges() hidden computation is widespread across two model classes. These substantive issues — combined with the undocumented IllegalStateException escape from a declared-IOException method — place the codebase below 60 on this dimension.

strengths: The four-phase exploration pipeline in SemanticFieldExplorer is decomposed into clearly named private helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounCollocateMap, scoreAndFilterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates), making each phase easy to reason about independently.
CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery explicitly labels its three phases with comments (Phase 1: collect, Phase 2: score, Phase 3: rank), leaving no ambiguity about the intended control flow.
Guard clauses at method entry (null/empty lemma, null pattern) return early before complex logic, keeping the happy path unobstructed.
BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution throws IllegalArgumentException for unrecognised formats rather than silently proceeding, making the failure mode explicit.
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are mild redundancies and structural oversights (orphaned comment, duplicated puts, double-clamping) rather than correctness defects; no code path provably does the wrong thing.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is largely correct and readable. The issues found — a duplicate branch sub-expression, a double null guard, an orphaned Javadoc, a redundant clamp, and re-computed layout constants — are real but local in scope and none breaks observable behaviour. This puts the codebase solidly above the median (50) but below the 'near-zero defects' range (85+); a score of 72 reflects good overall clarity with a handful of avoidable redundancies.

strengths: The QueryExecutor interface and its implementation use strongly-typed domain records (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, ConcordanceResult) as return types rather than raw maps for query results.
RelationConfig is a sealed record with typed fields; Optional<RelationType> is used deliberately to distinguish 'absent' from 'invalid' relation type entries.
GrammarConfigLoader validates config shape at load time, throwing typed IOExceptions for missing fields rather than deferring failures to first use.
PosGroup and RelationType are proper enums used in the config and query layers.
type_safety.issue_character: The dominant pattern is raw String used where a small enum or typed wrapper would close gaps — Edge.type, relation alias keys — combined with untyped Map<String,Object> for the entire API response surface.
type_safety.score_rationale: The core domain (query execution, config parsing, model records) is well-typed. The API boundary layer reverts to Map<String,Object> throughout, and a handful of string-typed discriminators that are functionally enumerations escape the type system. This is typical for JSON-over-HTTP Java without a dedicated DTO layer, but it leaves the widest code surface (all handlers) unprotected by types. A score of 69 reflects good discipline in the core with a significant gap at the boundary.

strengths: Domain terminology is used consistently: 'lemma', 'collocate', 'logDice', 'headword', 'deprel' appear with the same meaning throughout the codebase, reducing vocabulary switching cost.

Phase-helper methods in SemanticFieldExplorer have descriptive names that map directly to the exploration algorithm stages: fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounCollocateMap, scoreAndFilterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates.
Record types in the model package (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, ExploreOptions) are precisely named after their domain concepts.
Utility classes follow a clear convention: LogDiceCalculator, PatternSubstitution, CqlUtils — each name predicts the file contents accurately.
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are isolated to field names that are too generic for their types ('helper', 'comparator'), one local variable that diverges from the HTTP parameter name it wraps, and a method-naming inconsistency at the QueryExecutor interface boundary.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates genuine naming discipline at the model and utility layers. Weaknesses are concentrated in a few field names within complex orchestration classes where a generic name was chosen for convenience. No method name actively misleads about behaviour (no mutation-in-getter pattern, no non-boolean is*() methods). A score of 74 reflects good overall naming with a small cluster of avoidable generic identifiers.

strengths: The wrapHandler pattern in WordSketchApiServer provides a consistent last-resort boundary: all unhandled exceptions produce a 500 JSON response rather than hanging the connection.
IOException is consistently propagated through the query layer (BlackLabQueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper) with cause chaining, preserving the original stack trace.
ExplorationHandlers handlers distinguish IOException (500) from IllegalArgumentException (400) at each handler's call site, giving clients actionable HTTP status codes.
error_consistency.issue_character: The dominant failure pattern is swallowed exceptions at integration seams: missing config in Main, silently-partial results in the full-sketch handler, and silent discard of malformed query parameters.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The query core propagates errors faithfully, but the startup path and several API handlers trade correctness for resilience without informing the caller. The most serious issue — passing null grammarConfig after catching IOException — creates a deceptive 'using defaults' message followed by a hard crash, which is worse than simply propagating the original error. A score of 60 reflects a codebase where error strategy is sound in the core but breaks down at key integration boundaries.

strengths: All major components (BlackLabQueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateQueryHelper, GrammarConfigLoader) follow a constructor-injection pattern with no hidden global singletons outside RadialPlot.
GrammarConfigLoader reads the system property at call time rather than at class load, keeping the config path dynamic and testable.
BlackLabConllUIndexer properly uses AtomicLong for its counters, showing awareness of concurrency at class-field level.
The shutdown hook in Main.handleServerCommand explicitly sequences resource teardown (stop server, then close executor), preventing use-after-close.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The codebase's main initialization-coupling problem is a single class (RadialPlot) with a thread-unsafe static field, combined with a two-phase construction in WordSketchApiServer that leaves an object partially usable after its constructor returns.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Constructor injection is used consistently and there are no import-time I/O calls or module-level global singletons beyond the mutable RadialPlot formatters. The two-phase WordSketchApiServer construction is a moderate lifecycle smell but is contained and clearly documented in a TODO. The DecimalFormat thread-safety issue is a latent concurrency bug activated under production load. A score of 72 reflects a generally clean initialization design with one latent concurrent-access defect and one lifecycle asymmetry.

strengths: Uniform Java naming conventions (camelCase/PascalCase/UPPER_SNAKE_CASE) with no drift detected across any package.
Package-private visibility is consistently applied to internal handler classes, keeping the public API surface small and intentional.
The record vs traditional-class divergence in the model package is explicitly self-documented with rationale, making it easy to understand for future contributors.
Static utility classes all follow the same private-constructor singleton-prevention pattern.
convention_outlier.issue_character: The main convention irregularity is a within-class style island: the surface vs DEP branch of handleFullSketchForType builds response maps with different key schemas and shapes, and a stale orphaned Javadoc block in SemanticFieldExplorer creates a minor but confusing documentation artifact.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase scores well on inter-file convention consistency — naming, visibility, and package layout are disciplined throughout. Two localised issues prevent a higher score: the surface/DEP response shape asymmetry inside SketchHandlers (which a developer consuming the API would notice immediately) and the orphaned comment in SemanticFieldExplorer. Neither is pervasive, which keeps the score above average but short of 80+.

strengths: Core business logic (logDice, snippet parsing, CQL token splitting, grammar config loading) is thoroughly unit-tested with clear assertions.
Handler tests use a lightweight mock HttpExchange, enabling fast validation of all error paths (400/405 responses) without a running server.
SemanticFieldExplorer tests use a proper StubExecutor, correctly decoupling exploration algorithm tests from the real index.
Config validation (RelationsValidationTest) tests the grammar JSON schema independently of the query engine.
test_strategy.issue_character: Critical entry-point and data-pipeline code (Main CLI, ConlluConverter, RadialPlot) has no test coverage, and the integration tests for BlackLab execution likely require a physical index that may not be available in CI, making the test suite structurally incomplete for the most error-prone paths.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The utility and config layers are well-tested, which is good. However, the critical data-flow path — corpus ingestion (ConlluConverter), CLI argument handling (Main), and SVG output (RadialPlot) — lacks any tests. The blacklab integration tests' reliability depends on index availability. These gaps represent the highest-risk untested code in a research tool where indexing mistakes are silent and hard to detect. The score reflects solid coverage at the unit level but significant gaps at the system boundary.

strengths: Error responses are consistent across all endpoints: always a JSON object with a single 'error' key and an appropriate HTTP status code.
CORS and method-enforcement (registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler) are applied uniformly at the routing layer rather than per-handler.
The explore endpoints share buildBaseExploreResponse and buildExploreResponseBody helpers, keeping the shared sections of their response envelopes identical.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The API surface suffers from incremental growth without a unifying schema: each sub-system (sketch, exploration, semantic-field comparison, BCQL) was added with its own response shape, leaving a patchwork of inconsistent envelope fields (missing 'status', differing defaults, silent fallback in one endpoint only) that a frontend developer must learn case by case.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The error envelope is consistent, which is the baseline contract, but the success envelope is not. Missing 'status' in BCQL, divergent defaults between sibling exploration endpoints, and the QueryExecutor interface's overlapping method pair are genuine friction points for any developer consuming or extending the API. The score reflects a codebase that has good individual modules but no API contract enforcement across them.

strengths: Loopback-only binding provides a consistent, correctly-applied network-level isolation boundary.
Relation ID validation against the grammar config is applied uniformly across all structured endpoints, preventing unknown pattern injection.
CqlUtils.escapeForRegex is applied consistently to user-supplied lemma values before CQL embedding.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The tool is internally consistent in having no authentication (appropriate for a local research tool), but the open-ended /api/bcql endpoint deliberately bypasses the structured validation that all other endpoints apply — creating an inconsistent trust boundary: every other endpoint restricts the query space via grammar config validation, while BCQL accepts arbitrary patterns without restriction.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: For a local tool binding to loopback, the absence of auth is appropriate and consistently applied. The score is held below 75 by two genuine inconsistencies: the CORS origin is a repeated string literal rather than a constant (minor), and more significantly, /api/bcql is an unconstrained bypass of the query validation that all structured endpoints share. If this tool were ever exposed beyond localhost, the BCQL endpoint would be the sole unguarded attack surface. The CORS comment itself acknowledges future-proofing is needed.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper cleanly separates the three phases of BCQL execution (parse/search, collect hits, score), with each phase as a named private method.
The ExploreOptions record cleanly groups all tuning parameters for the exploration algorithm, avoiding a long argument list.
buildBaseExploreResponse and buildExploreResponseBody in ExplorationHandlers are correctly extracted as shared helpers across both explore endpoints.

SemanticFieldExplorer is split from AdjectiveCollocateRanker to keep individual class sizes manageable — the comment in AdjectiveCollocateRanker explains why ('extracted to keep SemanticFieldExplorer below 400 lines').
design_coherence.issue_character: The primary design issue is repeated structural duplication at the branching points: the boolean-flag anti-pattern in handleFullSketchForType, duplicated bracket-scanning logic between GrammarConfigLoader and CqlUtils, and duplicated two-phase seed-collocate accumulation between exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles — each point where a common abstraction should have been applied but wasn't.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase shows awareness of design concerns (extracted helpers, ExploreOptions record, phase-separated BCQL pipeline), but there are several places where that discipline breaks down under the pressure of adding new features. The boolean-flag method in SketchHandlers, the public internal overload in SemanticFieldExplorer, and the duplicated seed-accumulation pattern are each individually fixable but together indicate a pattern of incremental addition without refactoring back to coherence. The score reflects a codebase that is partially well-designed with visible regressions in specific hotspots.

issues.summary: Corpus-format tagset patterns hardcoded inside exploration layer instead of sourced from config
issues.suggestion: Add optional reverse-lookup pattern fields to RelationConfig (e.g. noun_pattern and collocate_pattern) and pass them through ExploreOptions or RelationConfig. Exploration classes should read from config, eliminating the tagset constants.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer: private static final String NOUN_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]";
AdjectiveCollocateRanker: private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]";
These Penn Treebank xpos values are a corpus-format decision; the exploration layer should receive them from RelationConfig or a config parameter rather than hardcoding them.

issues.summary: Main passes null grammarConfig to WordSketchApiServer after advertising graceful fallback, causing NPE
issues.suggestion: Either (a) create a GrammarConfigLoader.defaultConfig() factory that returns a built-in default config and call it on load failure in Main, or (b) make WordSketchApiServer tolerate a null grammarConfig by building a default loader internally.
evidence: Main.handleServerCommand: catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("No grammar config found at '" + grammarConfigPath + "', using defaults."); } then WordSketchApiServer server = new WordSketchApiServer(executor, port, grammarConfig); with grammarConfig still null.
WordSketchApiServer constructor: this.grammarConfig = Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, "grammarConfig must not be null"); — throws NullPointerException at runtime.
The user-facing message 'using defaults' is contradicted by the immediate NPE in the constructor.

issues.summary: AdjectiveCollocateRanker extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer for size reasons rather than domain cohesion
issues.suggestion: Reintegrate AdjectiveCollocateRanker into SemanticFieldExplorer (accepting the line count) or redefine the boundary by responsibility: if comparison is a genuinely distinct capability, expose it with a clear entry point rather than through a delegating passthrough.
evidence: AdjectiveCollocateRanker Javadoc: 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold.'
SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles is a one-line delegation: return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seeds, minLogDice, topCollocates);
AdjectiveCollocateRanker is public but its only caller is SemanticFieldExplorer via the comparator field — it is not an independently usable subsystem concept.

issues.summary: model package uses two different accessor conventions (records vs JavaBeans getters) without consistent rule
issues.suggestion: Standardize on Java records for immutable result types. ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult can be converted to records now that they are built in a single constructor call; the 'may grow over time' rationale in ExplorationResult is not borne out by current usage.
evidence: ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult use getNouns(), getAllAdjectives(), getSeed(), getSeedCollocates(), etc.
AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExploreOptions use record component accessors adjective(), nounScores(), sharedCollocates(), etc.
The Javadoc in ComparisonResult documents the inconsistency as intentional, but a developer must still switch reading patterns within the same package.

issues.summary: Domain object serialization logic co-located in HTTP handler classes rather than in model or a dedicated serializer
issues.suggestion: Move formatXxx methods to a dedicated ExplorationResponseBuilder class or add toMap()/toResponseMap() methods to the domain records, keeping handler methods focused on request parsing and dispatch.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers contains formatSeedCollocate, formatDiscoveredNoun, formatCoreCollocate, formatEdge, formatAdjectiveProfile — 7 private static formatter methods each returning Map<String,Object>.
These methods encode which fields appear in the JSON response and how they are named, which is a separate concern from routing and parameter validation.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles is a zero-value passthrough to AdjectiveCollocateRanker
issues.suggestion: Remove the delegation by either (a) making AdjectiveCollocateRanker directly accessible from ExplorationHandlers (inject it as a collaborator), or (b) reintegrating AdjectiveCollocateRanker into SemanticFieldExplorer so the single-class explorer is the canonical entry point without empty forwarding.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles body: return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seeds, minLogDice, topCollocates);
No pre-processing, validation, transformation, or post-processing is added by SemanticFieldExplorer over AdjectiveCollocateRanker for this path.

ExplorationHandlers calls semanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles(...) — if SemanticFieldExplorer adds no value, the handler could call AdjectiveCollocateRanker directly or the ranker should be merged back.

issues.summary: Tagset-specific POS patterns hardcoded in exploration classes instead of sourced from config
issues.suggestion: Add nounPattern and adjectivePattern fields to RelationConfig or ExploreOptions (or a dedicated config class) and source them from the grammar config JSON at startup. Pass the resolved patterns into SemanticFieldExplorer via constructor or ExploreOptions.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer: private static final String NOUN_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]";
AdjectiveCollocateRanker: private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]";
These constants are configuration values (Penn Treebank xpos patterns) embedded as implementation constants in logic classes; changing the tagset requires editing exploration code.

issues.summary: Domain-to-map serialization methods embedded as private static helpers in handler classes rather than on domain types
issues.suggestion: Add toResponseMap() methods to the relevant record/class types in the model package, or create a dedicated ExplorationSerializer class in the api package, moving all formatXxx logic there to keep ExplorationHandlers focused on HTTP concerns.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers has: formatSeedCollocate(String, double, long), formatDiscoveredNoun(DiscoveredNoun), formatCoreCollocate(CoreCollocate), formatEdge(Edge), formatAdjectiveProfile(AdjectiveProfile) — all returning Map<String,Object>.
These methods encode the JSON field names and rounding rules for domain types defined in the model package; they leak serialization knowledge into the handler layer.
No equivalent accessor exists on DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, etc. — the domain types are unable to describe their own wire representation.

issues.summary: BlackLab 4.0.0 resolved from GitHub raw URL repository, creating build reproducibility and availability risk
issues.suggestion: Publish the local BlackLab JAR to a proper repository (GitHub Packages, JitPack, or a self-hosted Nexus/Artifactory) or vendor the JAR into the project's local Maven repository directory and configure a local file:// repository path. Alternatively, check whether BlackLab 4.x is available on Maven Central.
evidence: pom.xml repository entry: <url>https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/</url>

issues.summary: Production HTTP server relies on com.sun.net.httpserver, a JDK-internal API not guaranteed across JDK distributions
issues.suggestion: Replace com.sun.net.httpserver with a lightweight, properly specified HTTP server dependency such as the JDK's com.sun.net.httpserver exposed via --add-exports (acceptable as a short-term shim) or migrate to a small embedded server like Jetty, Undertow, or the JEP 408 Simple Web Server (Java 18+).

com.sun.net.httpserver is an internal package present in HotSpot JDKs but not part of the Java SE specification; it may be unavailable on non-Oracle/OpenJDK distributions or restricted by future module encapsulation.
No maven dependency declares this; it is an implicit platform dependency.

issues.summary: LongIntHashMap uses Long.MIN_VALUE as empty-slot sentinel without documenting that this key cannot be stored

issues.summary: maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR in CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits can overflow for large maxResults

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.throwForMissingConfig is a void method that always throws but its name does not communicate unconditional exception
